coredata.dc:description: In this study, multifunctional green carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized using Galla chinensis waste (GCW) via hydrothermal method for the first time. An active packaging film has been developed in this work by combining CDs and pullulan (PL), using the solution-casting method. The microscopic morphology revealed that the CDs that were prepared using GCW exhibited good compatibility with PL. In addition, it also led to improvement in the toughness of the PL film (14.01 % to 20.26 %), along with its water vapor permeability value [1.31 to 0.53 (g·mm)/(kPa·h·m2)]. The composite films consisting of CDs exhibited good UV blocking rates for the UVA (90.41 %–7.87 %), UVB (87.76 %–0.08 %), and UVC (83.39 %–0 %) spectral ranges. The composite films exhibited strong antioxidant activity, and the clearance of ABTS and DPPH were obtained to be 93.61 % and 86.30 %, respectively. In addition, the composite films showed good antibacterial activity for E. coli and S. aureus, with a high antibacterial rate of up to 99.99 %. Finally, the non-contact preservation of strawberries over a duration of 10 d at room temperature confirmed that the prepared composite film can help preserve the quality of strawberries, as well as extended their shelf-life.

head.ce:title: Preparation of multi-functional active packaging film of Galla chinensis waste CDs/pullulan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, multifunctional green carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized using Galla chinensis waste (GCW) via hydrothermal method for the first time. An active packaging film has been developed in this work by combining CDs and pullulan (PL), using the solution-casting method. The microscopic morphology revealed that the CDs that were prepared using GCW exhibited good compatibility with PL. In addition, it also led to improvement in the toughness of the PL film (14.01 % to 20.26 %), along with its water vapor permeability value [1.31 to 0.53 (g·mm)/(kPa·h·m2)]. The composite films consisting of CDs exhibited good UV blocking rates for the UVA (90.41 %–7.87 %), UVB (87.76 %–0.08 %), and UVC (83.39 %–0 %) spectral ranges. The composite films exhibited strong antioxidant activity, and the clearance of ABTS and DPPH were obtained to be 93.61 % and 86.30 %, respectively. In addition, the composite films showed good antibacterial activity for E. coli and S. aureus, with a high antibacterial rate of up to 99.99 %. Finally, the non-contact preservation of strawberries over a duration of 10 d at room temperature confirmed that the prepared composite film can help preserve the quality of strawberries, as well as extended their shelf-life.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Strawberries are rich in nutrients and are acclaimed as the “queen of fruits”, containing abundant vitamins. However, their soft skin makes them highly prone to physical damage during harvesting, storage, and transportation. Additionally, strawberries are also susceptible to microbial contamination, which can lead to decay and spoilage. This results in a significant reduction of their shelf life, and they can usually be stored for only 2–3 d at room temperature [1,2]. Currently, traditional physical approaches being employed for the preservation of strawberries are either too costly or remain ineffective. On the other hand, chemical methods lack the required safety levels even though they offer the advantage of lower costs. Active food packaging films [3,4] are for maintaining the freshness of perishable food items, extending their shelf life, enhancing food safety, providing aesthetically pleasing packaging, promoting environmental sustainability, and improving food usability. They are thus an indispensable part of modern food industrial practices.

People are becoming increasingly aware of the non-degradable nature of plastic packaging owing to the constant and rapid societal development. This research is now being focused on finding alternative biodegradable biomaterials that can help limit the adverse effects they have on the environment. Pullulan (PL) is a water-soluble and extracellular polysaccharide, which is primarily composed of repeating units of maltotriose connected by α-1,4 glycosidic bonds and further polymerized through α-1,6 glycosidic bonds to form a linear polysaccharide. It is a natural, green, non-toxic, colorless, and tasteless polysaccharide, with excellent characteristics such as good film-forming ability, flexibility, and biodegradability [5,6]. It has low viscosity, does not age (the solubility of PL solution does not decrease during cooling), and can be easily shaped into different forms. It has found extensive use across diverse fields, including medicine [7], food [8], environmental applications [9], and optoelectronics [10]. However, the use of PL films also presents some problems such as weak antioxidant, antibacterial, and UV-blocking properties that limit their practical application in comparison to the traditional natural polymer packaging films. Several studies have been conducted in order to address and overcome these shortcomings, and to enhance their antibacterial properties by incorporating substances like ZnO [11], and Ag [12]. Nevertheless, these inorganic oxides tend to agglomerate if loading of the nanoparticles surpasses a critical point value, which affects their transparency in the visible light spectrum. Additionally, serious concerns regarding the long-term biocompatibility, as well as the potential toxicity of metals and semiconductor materials have been raised. In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained attention in the scientific community for their unique functional properties in applications such as packaging and preservation of food items [13].

CDs are a type of zero-dimensional carbon-based materials that consist of dispersed quasi-spherical carbon particles with highly minute sizes (<10 nm), which exhibit fluorescent properties. They were first characterized by Xu et al. [14] and have since gained immense interest in recent years owing to their excellent optical characteristics, wide range of raw material sources, low cost, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, water solubility, and excellent biocompatibility [15–18]. They find applications in various fields, including bioimaging [19], drug delivery [20], optoelectronic materials [21], and biomedical applications [22]. The surface of CDs contains various functional groups (including amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) and thus exhibits exceptional functional properties such as antimicrobial, antioxidant, high UV resistance, and luminescence.

Galla chinensis (GC) is an insect gall of the sumac family that is formed by Rhus gall aphids, parasitized on the leaves of Rhus potaninii Maxim, or Rhus chinensis Mill. It can yield an annual output of about 12,000 tons. The GC has been employed as a traditional Chinese medicinal remedy for >2000 years [23,24]. Its active components are collectively referred to as tannic acid (60–70 %). The tannic acid, is obtained from the GC after the crushing, extraction and purification processes and it belongs to the polyphenolic family of compounds. Tannic acid exhibits strong antioxidant and astringent properties. It finds applications in various industries including feed [25], dyeing [26], medicine [27], metallurgy [28], cosmetics [29], food [30], and environmental protection [31]. Consequently, the annual usage of tannic acid has been on the rise, leading to a year-on-year growth rate in the production of Galla chinensis waste (GCW). Currently, the generated GCW is being discarded, which causes both waste of a resource, as well as environmental pollution. The extracted waste contains lipid, mineral, and carbohydrate components, which are rich in biomass energy. This can be processed further and used for power generation. Research on the constructive utilization of GCW is still in its infancy, and so there is an urgent need to actively undertake studies on the possible strategies to recycle it. Transforming discarded materials into new nanomaterials, such as green CDs can be effective method of turning waste into a useful resource. Techniques employed for the management of biowaste resources such as watermelon peels [32], pomelo peels [33], sugarcane bagasse [34], tea residues [35], corn cobs [36], and spent coffee [13] can provide good insights into possible approaches that may be viable for the recycling of GCW. The composition of GCW contains about 70–80 % carbohydrates, making it a potential and abundant source of CDs materials. Therefore, this study employs a “bottom-up” approach to first extract CDs from the GCW and then thoroughly explore their structural along with optical properties. This not only adds value to the GC resources but also enables waste reduction and resource utilization, contributing to economic and social development as well as environmental protection.

In this study, biomass-GCW was chosen as a carbon source, and green hydrothermal carbonization technology was used to prepare CDs with fluorescent properties, and then the morphology and structure of CDs were characterized. Because the GCW contains polyphenols, it provides a basis for the preparation of functional CDs composites. As we all know, the barrier, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties of polysaccharide films are poor, and CDs can make up for the shortcomings of polysaccharides, while CDs can make up for the shortcomings of polysaccharides. Therefore, CDs and PL were mixed to prepare composite films by solvent casting, and then the antioxidant, antibacterial, UV barrier, water vapor permeability, structure, microstructure, thermal and mechanical properties of the composite films were characterized. Strawberry is rich in nutrition, but the shelf life is short. In order to prolong the shelf life of strawberry, the composite film was used to test the preservation of strawberry, and the physicochemical indexes of strawberry were measured after 10 d of storage at room temperature. Therefore, this study can not only solve the problem of preservation, but also make the utilization of GCW.

Galla chinensis waste was supplied by Wufeng Chicheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Pullulan (CAS: 9057-02-7) was acquired from McLean Biochemical Company, Limited (Shanghai, China), while the fresh strawberries were picked from the Beile ecological strawberry picking garden (Kunming, China). Glycerol was acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). −L929 cells, E. coli, and S. aureus were provided by Kunming Yan'en Technology Co., Ltd. All additional reagents were analytically pure.

The CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal technique as described in Ref. [37] with some modifications. A 10 g specimen of GCW was weighed and placed inside a hydrothermal reactor, containing 300 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, an additional 200 mL of deionized water was introduced, after which the reactor was sealed. The temperature of the reactor was set to 190 °C, and the reaction took 6 h to reach its completion. Thereafter the system was allowed to naturally return to room temperature. The reaction product was filtered through a 0.22 μm water-based sand core filter, and the filtrate was then transferred to a brown bottle. After calculation, the final concentration of the CDs was 22.5 mg/mL, thereafter, it was refrigerated at a temperature of 4 °C for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and PL composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of composite film utilized a solution casting technique, as described in Ref. [38]. The synthesis process started by dispersing 1 g of PL in 30 mL of distilled water. CDs solutions of different volumes (0, 5, 10, 15, and 20 mL) were added separately. Thereafter, 0.1 g of glycerol was introduced as a plasticizer, and the mixture was stirred at ambient temperature for a duration of 1 h. The volume was fixed at 50 mL. It was then poured into a plastic petri dish with dimensions of 14 cm × 14 cm, and dried at a temperature of 60 °C. Finally, the composite films were characterized after 24 h of exposure and conditioning in an environment with a relative humidity value of 50 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral characterization of CDs and composite films

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra of the prepared films were recorded using an F-4600, fluorescence spectrometer from HITACHI, which provided an excitation wavelength range spanning from 280 to 520 nm and the photomultiplier tube voltage (PTV) was set to 850 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and Raman spectra
ce:section.ce:para: Information about the functional groups was acquired using a TENSON2, FTIR routine spectrometer from Bruker, which operated in the range from 400 to 4000 cm−1. The CDs were characterized using KBr pellet and composite films were studied by ATR. The Raman spectra of CDs were recorded by a Raman spectrometer (Invia, Renishaw, England), and the laser excitation wavelength was set to 532 nm.

The prepared samples were also analyzed using UV–Vis spectrophotometry (Carry 50 UV–Vis, Agilent, USA), where full-wavelength (200–800 nm) absorption of the CDs solution, as well as a thorough investigation into the UV-blocking characteristics of the composite films was conducted.
The parameters of XRD (D8 ADVANC, Brooker, Germany) were set as Cu-Kα radiation, the step size was 0.02, and the scanning speed was 5°/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CDs and composite films

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CDs was studied using a JEM-2100F, TEM from JEOL. The samples were diluted using water and then dropped onto a copper grid for scanning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscope energy dispersive system (SEM-EDS)
ce:section.ce:para: The microscopic structure and elemental distribution of films were observed using a TESCAN MIRA LMS SEM-EDS system. The sample was fixed on a conductive adhesive and was gold-coated. The detector used was SE2.

The surface roughness of the composite films was observed with a Bruker Dimension Icon, AFM. Tapping mode was used for AFM measurements. The film sample was fixed on a glass slide, and the scanning range was 8 μm × 8 μm.

The crystal structure of CDs and the composite film was analyzed using a Ultima IV XRD setup with a Cu target (35 kV, 30 mA). The range of scanning was kept within 5–60° and the rate was set to 5°/min.
Surface functional groups and the content of various elements within the CDs were analyzed using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS with Al Kα radiation. The voltage was 12 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of CDs and composite films

ce:section.ce:para: The particle size of the CDs was measured using a nanoparticle size analyzer (Omni, Bruker, USA).
The WCAs of the samples were determined using a JC2000C contact angle measurement instrument (POWEAREACH, China). A 5 μL water droplet was placed on the sample [39].

The sample was enclosed within a glass container with 60 mL of water (diameter = 35 mm, depth = 100 mm). It was then weighed and placed in a chamber with regulated temperature with dry silica gel (0 % RH, 0 Pa, 30 °C). Ultimately, the alteration in cup weight was recorded over 2 to 12 h, and the obtained slope was linear [40]. The WVP [(g·mm)/(kPa·h·m−2)] was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
WVP =
WVTR × t
Δ P
where WVTR is the slope [g/(m2·h)]; t is the film thickness (mm), and Δ P is the pressure difference (Pa).

A colorimeter (Chroma meter 3nh, Shanghai, China) was utilized to characterize the color of the films, and ΔE was calculated using the following formula.

The mechanical properties of the composite films were evaluated using a texture analyzer (TMS-PRO, FTC, USA). The film was divided into several strips each with dimensions of 80 mm × 15 mm, and the tensile speed was adjusted to 10 mm/min. The formulas for calculating tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) are given in Eq. (3) and (4), respectively.
(3)

where F is the maximum breaking force (N); w is the width (mm);

is the original distance between grips (mm); and L is the deformed distance (mm).

Accurately weighed 5.0 mg of the film sample was subjected to testing in a crucible using a thermal gravimetric setup (TG STA2500, NETZSCH, Germany). The ramp rate was adjusted to 10 °C/min, where the temperature range was kept within 30 to 600 °C.

The antioxidant activity of the film was monitored through two methods, namely DPPH (2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl) and ABTS [2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)] radical scavenging assays [41,42]. 25.0 mg of film was weighed and dispersed in 3.0 mL of distilled water, subsequently, 100 μL of sample solution was mixed with 3.9 mL of DPPH solution (0.1 mM). The resulting solution was stirred briskly and was then left to stand in the darkness at room temperature for a duration of 30 min. Subsequently, the measurement of absorbance was conducted at a wavelength of 517 nm. The DPPH value was determined utilizing the formula given in Eq. (5).
(5)
DPPH % =
1 − A
A 0
× 100
where
A 0
represents the absorbance of DPPH, and
A 1
represents the absorbance of the mixture of DPPH and the film.

A solution with a concentration of 7.44 mM ABTS was combined in an equal proportion with a 2.6 mM potassium persulfate solution, and the blend was left to stand in darkness at room temperature for a duration of 24 h. The concentration of the resultant mixture was subsequently reduced by adding ethanol, and the absorbance of ABTS was measured at a wavelength of 734 nm. The sample preparation process first included dissolving 25.0 mg of the film in 45.0 mL of distilled water, kept in darkness at room temperature for 3 h to obtain the sample solution. Thereafter, 0.4 mL of the sample solution was combined with 3.6 mL of the ABTS solution, stirred well for 30 min, and subsequently allowed to stand for 6 min. The measurement of absorbance was conducted at a wavelength of 734 nm. The value of ABTS was obtained using the following formula.
(6)
ABTS % =
A 2
−
A 3
A 2
× 100
where
A 2
represents the absorbance of ABTS and
A 3
represents the absorbance of the mixture of ABTS and the film.

The antimicrobial performance of the mixed solution was assessed using the disc agar diffusion method [39]. Specifically, each prepared specimen (PL, PL-CDs1, PL-CDs2, PL-CDs3, and PL-CDs4) was separately added to sterile centrifuge tubes containing 5 mL of Luria-Bertani (LB) liquid culture medium. A negative control was established by adding a sterile LB liquid culture medium into another tube. Subsequently, each culture tube was filled with 50 μL of the activated and passaged bacterial solution, followed by cultivation on a shaker at a temperature of 37 °C and at 200 rpm for 24 h. Sterilized LB solid culture medium was then poured into sterile culture dishes. Following solidification of the medium, the overnight-treated bacterial cultures from each group were uniformly spread onto the LB solid culture medium after dilution to 106 times. The dishes were then inverted and incubated at a temperature of 37 °C for 24 h, and the number of bacterial colonies was determined as part of the statistical analysis.

The L929 cells in the logarithmic growth stage were inoculated with 1 × 104 cells/mL into 96-well plates, with each well inoculated with 100 μL cells. The pasteurized membrane materials of PL, PL-CDs1, PL-CDs2, PL-CDs3 and PL-CDs4 were drilled into the cell culture system with a hole punch (diameter = 5 mm). After 24 h of culture, 10 μL CCK8 reagent was added and was kept in an incubator at a temperature of 37 °C for 3 h [43]. The absorbance value of OD450 was determined and analyzed statistically.

The strawberries were harvested from a strawberry-picking garden in Kunming, Yunnan Province. The picked fruits were 90 % ripe, uniform in size, free from mechanical damage and diseases, and were immediately transported to the laboratory after being harvested. The strawberries were rinsed with distilled water followed by air-drying. The strawberries were randomly separated into four groups with 9 strawberries in each group. Three groups were sealed for preservation using the PE, PL, and PL-CDs3 films each having dimensions of 10 cm × 10 cm, respectively. The fourth group served as a blank control. The strawberries were preserved at ambient temperature for 10 d, and measurements were taken after every 2 d, with each group conducted in triplicate. The weight loss (WL), decay, firmness, total soluble solids (TSS) and titratable acid (TA) content of strawberries were determined to indicate the freshness of strawberries. The WL was calculated using the weighing method, with measurements taken every 48 h during the entire period of observation. Strawberries showing signs of wilting, mold growth, or rot were considered as decayed fruits, and the decay rate is the ratio of bad fruit to the total number of strawberries multiplied by 100 % to obtain the decay rate. The firmness of the fruits was determined using a texture analyzer with a testing speed of 5 mm/min, a compression depth of 15 mm, and a cylindrical probe having a diameter of 10 mm. 5 g of the homogenized strawberries were taken and added to 0.5 mL water for an ultrasound treatment of 30 min. After the centrifugation process, the TSS were found using an abbe refractometer (NAR-1 T SOLID, Atago, Japan). The TA in strawberries, represented by citric acid, was determined using an acid-base titration method [2]. The TA content was calculated according to the following formula.
(7)
TA
% Citric acid
=
v × 0.067 × 0.01
m
× 100
where v is the volume of NaOH used for titration (mL), 0.01 is the molarity of NaOH solution (mol/L), 0.067 is the reduction coefficient for malic acid, and m is the total weight of the cherries (g).

All data was obtained from a minimum of three parallel samples. Graphs and data processing were performed using ChemDraw 20.0 and Origin 2021. The Prism software was used to calculate the number of colonies in the bacteriostatic experiment, and the statistical method was the independent sample t-test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of CDs using FL, TEM, DLS, and UV–Vis characterizations
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs prepared using the one-step method were characterized using FS, TEM, DLS, and UV–Vis spectrophotometry. Because the concentration of the prepared solution was too high, the stock solution was diluted 5 times for subsequent fluorescence determination. The fluorescence spectra of the prepared CDs were acquired by employing an excitation wavelength spanning from 400 to 480 nm, with emission spectra intervals of 20 nm (see Fig. 1a), in order to examine their fluorescence properties. The maximum fluorescence intensity for the obtained CDs was observed at 503 nm with an excitation wavelength of 440 nm. This indicated that the fluorescence emission of the CDs prepared from the GCW exhibited a typical excitation wavelength-dependent response, where a redshift was observed at longer excitation wavelengths. Similar observations were reported in a research undertaken by Wagh et al., where preparing CDs were synthesized from Brassica oleracea [44]. Under visible light, the synthesized CDs appeared brownish-yellow in color, while under UV irradiation at a wavelength of 365 nm, they exhibited a blue color (see Fig. 1b). TEM images (Fig. 1c) revealed that the CDs exhibited a nearly spherical morphology and were well-dispersed. Also, they displayed a clear graphitic lattice structure with a lattice spacing value of 0.213 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of sp2 graphitic carbon [45]. The acquired DLS results (refer to Fig. 1d) showed a particle size variation between 4.09 nm and 5.02 nm, with a mean size of 4.47 nm. The findings of this characterization also indicated a uniform particle size distribution, which was consistent with the obtained TEM images and was within the specified range (<10 nm) for CDs. The obtained UV–Vis spectrum (see Fig. 1e) exhibited a typical absorption peak at a wavelength of 280 nm for the extracted CDs, which is in good agreement with the findings of the study undertaken by Li et al. [46]. This can be attributed to the n-π* transition of the CC bonds or the π-π* transition of the CO bonds, indicating the formation of a carbon structure in the graphite core. The quantum yield (QY) was determined according to the method of Zhang et al. [47]. Quinine sulfate was used as the standard (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, Ф = 54 % at 360 nm). The results showed that the QY of CDs was 9.16 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RXD, Raman spectrum, FTIR and XPS characterization of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD (Fig. 1f) showed that the prepared CDs has a wide unimodal peak at about 20.5°, indicating an amorphous structure [43]. The Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 1g) showed that there were two obvious peaks in the CDs material, which were located at 1372 cm−1 and 1585 cm−1, respectively, which are called D-band and G-band. Among them, the D-band is related to the crystal defects of the carbon material, while the G-band is related to the tensile vibration in the C-sp2 mixing plane, and the strength ratio ID/IG (peak height ratio) of the D-band and the G-band is related to the defect structure of the carbon material itself. The ID/IG value was 0.92, which indicated that there was a disordered structure or defect in the material [48]. The FTIR results (refer to Fig. 1h) revealed absorption peaks at 3273 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching and bending vibrations of NH, which indicated the presence of amide or amino groups on the surface of the CDs. The absorption peak at 3401 cm−1 was attributed to the OH stretching vibrations, while the peak found at 2964 cm−1 corresponded to bending vibrations of the unsaturated carbon CH bonds [49]. The absorption peak at 1760 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of CO, whereas the peak observed at 1234 cm−1 was associated to the CN stretching vibrations. Finally, the peak found at 1052 cm−1 indicated the stretching vibrations of the CO bonds [50]. The elemental composition and atomic percentages of the obtained CDs were investigated using XPS, the results of which are presented in Fig. 1i. The figure indicated that the CDs consisted of the elements C, N, and O with their respective percentages of 78.4 %, 4.0 %, and 17.6 %. The peak fitting of C1s (refer to Fig. 1j) revealed four distinct peaks associated with the CC/CH (284.46 eV), CN (285.03 eV), CO (286.30 eV), and CO (288.67 eV) bonds [51]. The spectrum obtained for N-1 s (see Fig. 1k) displayed peaks at 399.60 eV and 400.75 eV, which were linked to the CN and NH bonds, respectively. The spectrum for O1s (refer to Fig. 1l) showed two characteristic peaks at 532.05 eV and 533.21 eV for the OC and OC bonds, respectively. The XPS results agreed well with the FTIR analysis, indicating the existence of abundant surface functional groups that provided the CDs with good water dispersibility, enhanced optical performance, as well as other excellent material properties. The morphological characterization results aligned with the typical features reported for CDs, confirming the successful preparation of uniformly distributed CDs. One of the typical features of CDs is that they are zero-dimensional carbon-based spherical nanomaterials with a size of <10 nm; The second is that CDs have a small volume, and these CDs exhibit unique photoluminescence characteristics; The third is that CDs have abundant surface functional groups. These characteristics lead to CDs having functional characteristics such as antibacterial, UV resistance, and low toxicity, etc. [52,53].

The FTIR spectrum for PL, revealed a broad peak at 3292 cm−1 as shown in Fig. 2a, which aligned with the stretching vibration of the OH bonds. The peak found at 1652 cm−1was attributed to the OCO group, while the peak at 2927 cm−1 was ascribed to bending vibrations of the saturated carbon CH bonds. The absorption peaks at 990 cm−1, 851 cm−1, and 754 cm−1 corresponded to the α-(1,6) glycosidic bonds, α-pyranose glucose ring, and the α-(1,4) glycosidic bonds, respectively [8,54]. No new peaks were observed after formation of the composite films by the combination of PL with CDs, suggesting that no new chemical bonds were developed between PL and the CDs. The broadening of the absorption peak centered at 3292 cm−1 and the minor shift in the peak positions of the involved functional groups indicated the presence of hydrogen bonding occurring between the –OH groups of PL and the CDs, which is in accordance with prior research findings [13]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the spectral region of the film containing CDs had been slightly shifted, thereby confirming uniform integration and high compatibility between the CDs and the PL polymer matrix.

The XRD pattern obtained for the PL polysaccharide exhibited a broad peak at 2θ = 19.09°, as shown in Fig. 2b. This indicated the formation of a typical amorphous structure characteristic of a non-crystalline substance. This also suggested that the prepared PL polysaccharide was an amorphous solid with an internally disordered molecular arrangement, in line with the results reported by Liu et al. [55]. Furthermore, no additional peaks were obtained in the XRD pattern with the addition of the CDs. However, the intensity of the diffraction peaks was reduced. This implied that the presence of CDs interfered with the intermolecular hydrogen bonding of the PL biopolymers. However, the addition of CDs in a small quantity did not significantly alter the crystal structure of the PL matrix. This observation indicates the simultaneous presence of CDs and the PL biodegradable polymers [56].

The thermal property test was utilized to assess the heat sealing performance of the synthesized film samples. The thermal degradation of PL took place in three main stages, as shown in Fig. 2c. In the first stage, the loss in mass below a temperature of 100 °C was primarily attributed to the evaporation of water. The second stage corresponding to a peak degradation temperature of 259 °C was ascribed to glycerol degradation, whereas the third stage, with a peak decomposition temperature of 305.3 °C was primarily linked to the thermal degradation of the PL polymer chains, consistent with previous studies on PL-based films [57]. The results clearly suggested that the incorporation of CDs had little impact on the decomposition temperature of the composite films despite the hydrophilic characteristics of the CDs. These results suggested that the inclusion of CDs did not have a huge influence on the thermal stability of the PL film [58].

Mechanical properties reflect the strength and toughness of the film, and the factors that influence them are closely related to the magnitude of the intermolecular forces, as well as the microstructure of the film. The results presented in Fig. 2e and f clearly show the excellent mechanical performance of the pure PL film. This can be ascribed to the abundance of internal hydrogen bonds present within it. The inclusion of CDs shad a notable impact on the mechanical characteristics of the pure PL film. In comparison to the pure PL film, the values of TS and EB for the composite films with different components varied between 25.21 MPa and 19.63 MPa and from 14.01 % to 20.26 %, respectively. The value of TS for the film decreased, whereas the EB value increased significantly after introduction of the CDs. This can be attributed to the hydrophilicity of the CDs, leading to an elevated water content of the film and a reduction in the TS value. Additionally, CDs taken as small molecules can act as plasticizers when mixed with PL, and enhance the mobility of the PL polymer segments, thereby increasing the toughness of the composite films and reducing its rigidity, as reported in a study by Bao et al. [59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, WCA, WVP, and color analysis
ce:section.ce:para: It is evident from the values provided in Table 1 that the addition of CDs resulted in a notable increase in the water content of the composite film. This can be attributed to the fact that CDs were nano-sized carbon materials with strong hygroscopic properties, and their surfaces contained numerous hydrophilic groups, allowing them to absorb moisture from the surrounding environment. Hence, this resulted in an increase in their water content.

The WCA parameter provides a measure of the wetting properties of the surface of the film. It is often closely associated with the type and quantity of biopolymers and filler particles. The WCA of the pure PL film was measured to be 60.25°, indicating a hydrophilic surface (as WCA < 90°). A gradual reduction in the value of the WCA was noticed with an increase in the concentration of CDs in the solution. This reduction was associated to the abundance of hydrophilic groups present in the CDs, such as carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. This led to an enhancement in the hydrophilic properties of the CDs. Similar results have been reported when CDs were added to other polymer films [13,52].

The WVP parameter plays a crucial role in determining the barrier properties of materials used in food packaging. Packaging films with good barrier properties can offer reduced gas exchange between the interior and exterior sides of the packaging, slow down the proliferation of microorganisms, and thus extend the shelf life of food items. The transmission of water vapor in the film was influenced by various physical mechanisms, such as the adsorption of water vapor on the surface of the film, the diffusion of water molecules, and their evaporation on the opposite side of the film. The value of WVP for the pure PL film was calculated to be 1.31 (g·mm)/(kPa·h·m2). This value changed dramatically and decreased down to 0.53 (g·mm)/(kPa·h·m2) for the PL-CD4 specimen with an increase in the concentration of CDs. This further confirmed the presence of hydroxyl groups within the CDs that formed hydrogen bonds with PL, resulting in a decrease in the available free volume between the molecular chains of PL and an enhancement in gas tightness, as well as restricting the movement of water vapor molecules. Therefore, the incorporation of CDs led to a decrease in the WVP value of the PL film, which is in agreement with previous studies [60].

Synthesis of composite films through the introduction of functional components into the biopolymers can lead to changes in the visual appearance and optical characteristics of the composite films, depending on the interactions present between them. The pure PL film was transparent and colorless in appearance. However, the film gradually changed its appearance from colorless to yellow with the addition of CDs, which was determined by the color of the CDs itself [59]. The incorporation of CDs led to a decrease in the value of ΔL* for the PL film, indicating a reduction in brightness. On the other hand, the values of Δa* and Δb* were increased, indicating an increase in the yellow and red degrees of the composite film. This led to a notable rise in the ΔE value of the composite films.

The primary purpose of developing UV-blocking food packaging films is to shield food from UV damage, thereby extending its shelf life, as well as maintaining its quality. In recent years, UV-blocking packaging films have attracted increasing attention from researchers [61]. The transmittance of pure PL films and different amounts of CDs in the UV–Vis spectral range is shown in Fig. 3a. The transmittance of pure PL films, along with composite films of PL-CDs were investigated further at specific wavelengths in the UV range (UVA at 350 nm, UVB at 300 nm, and UVC at 250 nm). The original PL film exhibited UV transmittance rates of 90.41 %, 87.76 % and 83.89 % for UVA, UVB and UVC, respectively. With the addition of the CDs, the transmission rates for PL-CDs1, PL-CDs2, PL-CDs3 and PL-CDs4 at UVA were obtained to be 50.87 %, 27.79 %, 22.24 %, and 7.87 %, respectively. Similarly, the rates for these samples at UVB were found as 17.04 %, 3.84 %, 1.86 %, and 0.08 %, respectively, whereas at UVC the values were obtained to be 8.85 %, 1.21 %, 0.26 %, and 0 %, respectively. The addition of CDs resulted in excellent UV-blocking properties that almost completely blocked the transmission of UVB and UVC rays. The transmittance values for PL, PL-CDs1, PL-CDs2, PL-CDs3 and PL-CDs4 in the visible light region at 600 nm were determined to be 91.66 %, 89.00 %, 84.05 %, 83.71 % and 74.86 %, respectively. The introduction of CDs led to a slight reduction in the transparency within the visible light region (decreased 16.80 %). However, the patterns inside the composite film remained clearly visible, as shown in Fig. 3b. This indicated that the addition of CDs can provide comprehensive UV protection for the film without any significant loss in its transparency. One of the reasons for achieving UV barrier properties may be the size effect. The CDs displayed a higher specific surface area as a nanomaterial owing to the size effect, thus effectively absorbing and scattering the UV light. The surface atoms of nano CDs exhibited higher reactivity in comparison to the larger particles of carbon, making it easier to interact with UV light. Another possible reason might be related to the electronic structure of CDs, which significantly contributes to their ability to absorb UV light. The electrons in CDs can undergo energy level transitions upon absorption of UV photons, thereby achieving the function of blocking UV light [60,62]. Thus, the green CDs extracted from GCW proved to be excellent UV-absorbing nanomaterials that can effectively safeguard packaged goods from photooxidation and photodegradation reactions caused by UV exposure.

The characteristics of the raw materials employed in its synthesis impact the microstructure of the composite film. The crystalline structure, particle size, shape, compatibility, and other characteristics of different raw materials directly impact the microstructure of the composite film. The obtained surface (SF) and cross-sectional (CS) SEM images, along with the elemental distribution of the composite films are provided in Fig. 4 . The figure illustrates that the surface of the composite film (PL-CDs) displayed a seamless and uninterrupted structure, devoid of cracks or imperfections. No internal pores were found in the prepared film either. Also, the overall surface morphology of the composite film did not undergo any significant alterations as the concentration of added CDs was increased. The SEM images of the CDs also depicted the overall appearance of the film without any pores or fractures, indicating favorable compatibility between the CDs and the PL matrix [52]. In addition, the distribution of CDs in the PL matrix was further investigated by determining the percentage composition of C, N and O on the surface of the film using SEM-EDS characterization. The content of C and O in PL was found to be 65.69 % and 34.41 %, respectively. The presence of N in PL-CDs4 was linked to the addition of CDs, and the percentage contents of the three elements C, O and N were obtained as 65.50 %, 33.68 %, and 0.82 %, respectively. The images clearly showed a uniform distribution of elements, which suggests that the CDs were uniformly dispersed within the PL matrix. This may be attributed to the hydrogen bond interactions present among the abundant surface hydrophilic groups of the CDs and the PL polymer molecules. Additionally, the nanoscale particle characteristics of CDs allowed them to be dispersed and settle uniformly between the PL polymer molecules [31].

Further insights into the microstructure and nanoscale features of the prepared composite films were gained by studying their three-dimensional morphology using AFM. The three-dimensional image obtained within an 8 μm × 8 μm scanning range is shown in Fig. 5 , which intuitively revealed a positive correlation between the concentration of the CDs and the roughness of the films. The height of the film gradually grew with the addition of the CDs. This was in agreement with previous findings [63], where an increase in the nanoparticle content had led to aggregation that affected the roughness of the film.

Antioxidant activity refers to the ability of a material to eliminate free radicals. The free radical scavenging capacity of PL and its composite films was evaluated using the DPPH and ABTS techniques, the findings of which are presented in Fig. 6 . The figure illustrates that the pristine PL film exhibited antioxidant activities of 0.15 % and 0.73 % for DPPH and ABTS, respectively. This indicated minimal antioxidant activity [64]. The ability of the PL-CDs composite film to scavenge free radicals in the ABTS method increased from 0.73 % for PL to 93.61 % for the PL-CDs4 sample. On the other hand, the capacity to scavenge free radicals of the PL-CDs composite film for the DPPH test increased from 0.15 % for PL to 86.30 % for the PL-CDs4 specimen. The antioxidant activity of the composite film was found to be dependent primarily on the addition of CDs, as it elevated with the rise in the concentration of CDs. The film showed different antioxidant activities in the DPPH and ABTS solutions, possibly due to the hydrophilic characteristics of the CDs, the solubility of CDs in ABTS aqueous solution was higher than that in DPPH methanol solution. The antioxidant activity of the prepared CDs was related to the abundance of surface functional groups. The free radical scavenging mechanism of CDs has been widely discussed that DPPH and ABTS
+ can be reduced and quenched by the –OH groups DPPH-H and ABTS-H transfer H of CDs [65]. Similar studies on the development of active films with strong antioxidant properties upon the addition of CDs for packaging applications have also previously been reported [66,67].

Food packaging films with antibacterial properties can safeguard food from contamination by bacteria and other microorganisms, thereby extending the duration of shelf life while preserving the quality and safety of the food items. The antibacterial effects of different film-forming solutions on S. aureus and E. coli were investigated by employing the disc agar plate diffusion test [39]. The pure PL solution being used as a control sample did not exhibit antibacterial effects against S. aureus and E. coli, as depicted in Fig. 7 .

The incorporation of CDs enhanced the antibacterial properties of the composite films against both types of bacteria. The specimen PL-CDs4 almost completely restrained the proliferation of S. aureus and E. coli, as can be seen in Fig. 8 . The antibacterial effect of composite films containing low concentration CDs on S. aureus was significantly higher than that on E. coli. The reasons are that the cell wall chemical composition of Gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) is simple, generally containing only 90 % peptidoglycan and 10 % teichoic acid, while Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) contains lipopolysaccharide, phospholipids, outer membrane protein and a thin layer of peptidoglycan. It can provide additional protection, so the antibacterial effect of PL-CDs composite film on E. coli is significantly lower than that of S. aureus. One of the reasons for the antibacterial activity of CDs may be that CDs are used as nanoparticles, ultra-small nanoparticles can easily pass through the cell wall, directly destroy the cytomembrane of bacteria, change cell signals, stop DNA replication, interfere with metabolic activities, and lead to cell death [68]. The antibacterial properties of CDs are also related to a variety of factors, such as the charge, the functional groups and the photoinhibition properties of the CDs [39,46]. Therefore, the results suggest that applying these composite films for preserving fruits and vegetables can impede bacterial growth and their reproduction.

The cytotoxicity of food packaging films was evaluated to understand the effects of chemicals present in packaging materials on cells and thus to assess their overall impact on food safety. It was revealed that the cell activity initially followed an increasing trend and then decreased, with an increase in the concentration of CDs. This can clearly be seen in Fig. 9 . The results showed that the incorporation of CDs at lower concentrations resulted in heightened cell viability, and this proliferation could be attributed to the elevated hydrophilicity of the film. The subsequent reduction in cell viability may be ascribed to the relatively high content of CDs in the environment, which disrupted the proliferation of cells and damaged the organelles. However, the survival rate of cells exposed to CDs containing composite films was found to be higher than 86 % after 24 h, indicating that the toxicity of GCW CDs was small in vitro. Therefore, these results suggested that GCW CDs are quite safe as an antimicrobial agent for the preservation of food items. Other studies have also reported similar results [43,69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The composite films were used for the preservation of strawberries
ce:section.ce:para: Strawberries are highly perishable fruits, quite prone to losing their freshness rapidly. A novel composite film was prepared to extend their shelf life, maintain freshness, as well as to prevent them from decaying within a short period, and thus enhance the overall consumer experience [70]. Considering the barrier properties, mechanical properties, microstructure, chromaticity, and biocompatibility of the composite film, a fresh-keeping test was performed using the representative PL-CDs3 composite film. The freshness of the strawberries was assessed by measuring their WL rate, decay rate, firmness, TSS, and TA values during the storage process. The appearance of strawberries kept at room temperature for a duration of 10 d is shown in Fig. 10 .

Strawberries are rich in water with an average water content of around 90 %. The respiration and transpiration processes continuously result in the loss of water and weight reduction of the fruit after it is harvested, thereby affecting its physiological metabolism, as well as visual quality. Water retention is a key consideration for preserving strawberries. According to the results illustrated in Fig. 11a, both the control and the experimental groups exhibited an increasing trend in the WL rate of the strawberry quality as the storage time was extended. However, the experimental group revealed a significantly lower WL rate in comparison to the control group. The WL rate of the strawberry samples in the blank group reached a high value of 12.96 % on the 10th day, while the PE, PL and PL-CDs3 films exhibited WL rates of 0.38 %, 7.47 % and 6.65 %, respectively. This suggested that incorporating CDs in the PL film significantly inhibited the evaporation of water from the strawberry fruits, thus reducing water loss and demonstrating a certain preservation effect. The film treatment was therefore effective in reducing surface water evaporation and oxygen permeability. This aided in the suppression of respiratory processes, thereby minimizing water loss and organic matter consumption in the strawberries.

Strawberries are soft, juicy, and highly perishable during storage, leading to a decline in their sensory quality and even their edibility [1]. The study revealed that each coated treatment helped in improving the sensory quality of the strawberries during storage when compared to the uncoated control samples, as shown in Fig. 11b. The appearance of the strawberry samples in the control group became dark red, soft, and rotten, with plush mold, after 10 d of storage. The decay rate was determined to be 50.0 % by this time. The rot rate of the PE film was as high as 61.1 %, which may be attributed to the low permeability of the PE film, thus blocking the flow of air and moisture, and providing favorable conditions for the growth of mold. The decay rates of the PL and PL-CDs3 films were found as 38.9 % and 27.8 %, respectively. These values indicated that the film with CDs exhibited a better fresh-keeping effect and could slow down the decay rate of strawberries.

The change in firmness is closely related to the ripening process of fruits. The cell walls of fruits are filled with substances such as pectin and cellulose, and the breakdown or transformation of these substances may lead to softening of the fruits. Firmness tests are often performed to assess the texture of fruits during their storage and transportation [1,71]. The results presented in Fig. 11c clearly showed deterioration in the firmness of strawberries during the preservation process. This can be attributed to the activity of pectinase and cellulase, which may lead to the degradation of these complex polysaccharides, thereby reducing the firmness of cell walls and resulting in decreased firmness. The firmness of the control group was measured to be 5.40 N after 10 d, whereas its value for the PE, PL and PL-CDs3 film groups was found to be 5.40 N, 5.16 N and 6.02 N, respectively. The firmness of the PL-CDs3 film group showed a notable increase compared to the other groups. This indicated that the addition of CDs inhibited the respiratory activity of strawberries and slowed down the metabolism rate. This led to a smaller reduction in firmness of the strawberries, thus slowing down the rate of their softening.

Strawberries undergo respiration during storage and consume soluble sugar, as well as other organic substances present in the fruit. This leads to the generation of carbon dioxide and water, which leads to a reduction in the value of TSS [2]. The TSS value for the control group was determined to be 7.9 % after 10 d, while its value for the PE, PL and PL-CDs3 groups was found to be 8.7 %, 8.9 %, and 9.0 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. 11d. The rate of reduction in the value of TSS for strawberries that were preserved using the composite film was notably lower compared to the control group. This was mainly attributed to the effective inhibition of physiological metabolism of the strawberries by the film treatment, which delayed their ripening, and thus suppressing the consumption of soluble sugars caused by the respiratory activity. Conversely, the control group exhibited a high metabolism rate with significant consumption of soluble sugars and organic acids. The addition of CDs may generate reactive oxygen species, slowing down the aging of strawberries to some extent. This clearly showed that the composite film can effectively slow down the reduction in the content of TSS.

Strawberries undergo respiration during the storage process, leading to the consumption of some organic substances, including sugars and organic acid [33]. The results presented in Fig. 11e showed that the TA content of all strawberries continued to decrease as the storage time was extended. Among them, the control group exhibited the fastest decline, at 0.37 g/100 g, followed by the PE group at 0.49 g/100 g, the PL group at 0.43 g/100 g, and the PL-CDs3 group at 0.53 g/100 g. The group in which the CDs were added performed significantly better compared to the other groups. This may be attributed to the prepared composite film, which provided the strawberries with appropriate breathability, thereby reducing oxygen in the microenvironment between the fruit and the film. This resulted in a slowing down of the respiratory activity in the strawberries during their storage. Therefore, the longer the storage time of strawberries, the less was the TA content, which revealed that inhibiting respiration can reduce the consumption of TA.

The GCW is a kind of solid waste, which is the residue (30–40 %) after extracting tannic acid from GC (annual output of 13,000 tons), and its water content is as high as 90 %. At present, the GCW is discarded, causing environmental pollution and waste of resources. In the context of green and sustainable development, people are paying more and more attention to environmental issues. Traditional plastic packaging materials have caused serious ecological problems due to their non-biodegradability, so bio-based packaging materials have attracted more and more researchers ‘attention. Unfortunately, no polymer can completely block the gas, and most polymers allow UV light to pass through. However, many foods not only need to be isolated from gas during storage, but also need to be isolated from UV light to maintain their freshness. PL is an extracellular polysaccharide with good biocompatibility, biodegradability and non-toxicity, so it has applications in the fields of food, medicine, etc. However, compared with the traditional natural polymer packaging film, the PL film has the disadvantages of anti-oxidation, antibacterial and weak UV barrier, which limits the wide application of PL film. In order to turn waste into treasure and develop the utilization direction of GCW, the research on the preparation of green CDs from biomass waste has attracted our attention. Due to its extremely small size and adjustable functional properties, CDs have been popular in the field of food packaging. In this study, GCW was synthesized and characterized by a simple bottom-up synthesis method. The results showed that the CDs prepared by GCW emitted blue light under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, with uniform particle distribution and rich functional groups. PL was mixed with different proportions by solution-casting and dried to form a film. After the addition of CDs, the WVP value of the prepared composite films decreased, indicating that hydrogen bonds were formed between CDs and PL, which improved the air tightness. The increase in antioxidant properties is because CDs have active groups, most of which can be used as proton donors to scavenge free radicals after reacting with reactive oxygen species. The addition of CDs has antibacterial activity because CDs, as nanoparticles, are easy to enter cells and destroy cell metabolism. The UV barrier performance of the composite film is significantly increased, because the electrons in the CDs can undergo energy level transition when absorbing ultraviolet photons, thereby achieving UV absorption. It can be seen that the composite film can effectively protect food from UV-induced deterioration. The cytotoxicity test showed that the in vitro toxicity of CDs was very small, indicating that the composite membrane had higher biosafety to organisms. Finally, the composite film is used for the preservation of strawberry. Strawberry is a very popular fruit, which is favored for its unique flavor, bright color and rich nutritional value. However, strawberry is also a very delicate fruit, which is easy to rot and lose freshness after picking. Therefore, preservation is very important for strawberries. The experimental results showed that the prepared composite film can extend the preservation of strawberry to 10 d. The reasons were as follows. First of all, the antibacterial property of the composite film protects the appearance of strawberry fruit from external scratching and pollution. Secondly, the film acts as an invisible barrier, and the lower WVP slows down the pace of water escape and oxidation inside the strawberry, effectively delaying the softening and decay of the fruit. Furthermore, the UV barrier of the film reduced the erosion of precious vitamins and antioxidants in strawberries by light and oxidation, and retained the nutritional essence and unique flavor of strawberries. Finally, the film can also subtly regulate the gas flow inside the strawberry, providing a suitable environment for the respiration and metabolism of the strawberry. Therefore, the composite film shows a potential application prospect in strawberry preservation, and provides a solution to improve the storage and transportation effect of agricultural products. This study is only from the preparation of GCW into nanomaterials and polysaccharides to prepare composite films and used for the preservation of strawberries. The next work is to explore new uses, develop more use directions for the use of GCW, and increase the added value of GC.

This study utilized GCW as a carbon source to synthesize biomass CDs through a hydrothermal synthesis method. The CDs exhibited a maximum emission wavelength of 503 nm for an excitation wavelength of 440 nm, an average particle size of 4.47 nm, with a uniform particle size distribution, and a surface rich in various functional groups. The PL-CDs composite films were synthesized through a solvent casting method using PL as the film-forming matrix. The prepared composite films were then thoroughly characterized and examined using FTIR, TG, XRD, WVP, SEM-EDS, AFM, UV–Vis, WCA, antioxidant, and antibacterial analyses. The results indicated that the obtained CDs exhibited good compatibility with PL. The toughness of the PL film added with CDs increased from 14.01 % to 20.26 %, an increase of 44.61 %. The WVP decreased from 1.31 to 0.53 (g·mm)/(kPa·h·m2), which decreased by 59.54 %. The addition of CDs almost completely blocked the transmission of UVB (10.04 %–0.08 %) and UVC (8.85 %–0 %) rays, and the UV blocking performance was significantly increased. The antioxidant activity of the composite films against ABTS (0.73 %–93.61 %) and DPPH (0.15 %–86.30 %) increased, and the antibacterial rate against E. coli and S. aureus was >99.99 %. Finally, PL-CDs3 composite film was used for strawberries preservation at room temperature, and the composite film with CDs could effectively extend the shelf life of strawberries for 10 d. In summary, the active composite film developed in this study can be prepared using a simple and convenient synthesis process and by employing environmentally friendly raw materials. The synthesized film holds great potential for the preservation of fruits and vegetables. Additionally, it opens up new pathways for the effective utilization of GCW in novel applications.

Baoshan Tang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Xi Wu: Validation, Investigation. Lanxiang Liu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Juan Xu: Validation, Methodology. Jinju Ma: Validation, Conceptualization. Hong Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
